title: Quiz: Dynamical Decoupling
description: Test your understanding of dynamical decoupling techniques for protecting quantum coherence

questions.question: What is the basic principle behind dynamical decoupling?

options.text: Cooling the environment to reduce noise
Using pi-pulses to invert accumulated phase errors
Measuring and correcting errors in real-time
Isolating the qubit from all interactions

questions.explanation: Dynamical decoupling uses pi-pulses to invert the sign of accumulated phase errors. Two consecutive periods with opposite signs cancel out, effectively refocusing the dephasing.

questions.question: What is the Hahn echo sequence?

options.text: Four alternating X and Y pulses
A single pi-pulse at the midpoint
Continuous wave irradiation
A sequence of increasing pulse amplitudes

questions.explanation: The Hahn echo is the simplest DD sequence: free evolution for t/2, one pi-pulse, then free evolution for t/2. It refocuses quasi-static (slowly varying) noise.

questions.question: Why does CPMG use multiple evenly-spaced pulses?

options.text: To make the sequence longer
To suppress noise that varies on timescales shorter than the sequence
To reduce the total number of pulses needed
To eliminate the need for precise timing

questions.explanation: Multiple pulses in CPMG provide protection against noise that varies on timescales comparable to or shorter than the pulse spacing. More pulses = better suppression of higher-frequency noise.

questions.question: XY sequences alternate between X and Y pulses to suppress pulse errors.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. XY sequences like XY-4 and XY-8 alternate between X and Y pulses. This helps cancel systematic pulse errors while maintaining decoherence protection.

questions.question: What does the filter function F(omega) describe?

options.text: The noise spectrum of the environment
How the DD sequence suppresses noise at different frequencies
The coherence time of the qubit
The pulse shape in the time domain

questions.explanation: The filter function F(omega) describes the frequency response of a DD sequence - it shows how well the sequence suppresses noise at each frequency. Low F(omega) means good suppression.

questions.question: For quasi-static noise (slowly varying), which statement is true?

options.text: A single Hahn echo provides nearly perfect refocusing
Many pulses are required
DD cannot help
Only UDD sequences work

questions.explanation: For quasi-static (slowly varying) noise, a single Hahn echo pulse at the midpoint is sufficient for nearly perfect refocusing, since the noise barely changes during the sequence.

questions.question: Which are practical limitations of dynamical decoupling? (Select all)

options.text: Finite pulse duration
Pulse imperfections
Heating from the pulses
Requirement for infinite pulses

questions.explanation: Real DD implementations face finite pulse duration, pulse imperfections (rotation errors), and heating effects. While more pulses help, infinite pulses are not required - practical sequences use a finite number.

questions.question: How does Uhrig DD (UDD) differ from CPMG?

options.text: UDD uses X pulses instead of Y pulses
UDD has non-uniform pulse spacing optimized for low-frequency noise
UDD requires more pulses
UDD only works at zero temperature

questions.explanation: Uhrig DD places pulses at non-uniform times given by t_j = T*sin^2(pi*j/(2n+2)), optimized to suppress low-frequency noise to higher order than uniform CPMG spacing.
